one_line_takeaway: A TARA-inspired threat-modeling framework and open-source tool for in-vehicle LLM agents that cleanly separates human-centric assets from graph-based attack paths and formalizes poison-vs-trigger paths, but is validated only by construction and illustrative examples, with no empirical evaluation of its own.
key_finding: The framework's main asserted result is qualitative/structural rather than measured: that formally separating assets from attack paths, plus recognizing the recursive poison-vs-trigger structure, enables systematic and generative threat enumeration (for each asset, enumerate paths; for each path, enumerate assets) that component-centric taxonomies cannot support. The authors argue a naive single-level search would treat every dormant payload as costing the same as an active propagation, dramatically overestimating attack complexity, whereas the bi-level architecture prices trigger paths as sub-problems. The recursive structure is illustrated (not empirically validated) against two real disclosed CVEs -- CurXecute in Cursor IDE and EchoLeak in Microsoft 365 Copilot -- both of which the authors show fit the two-stage poison-then-trigger pattern.
core_contribution: The paper proposes AgentHeLLM (Agent Hazard Exploration for LLM Assistants), a threat-modeling framework for LLM-based conversational agents deployed in vehicles. Its central methodological claim is a 'separation of concerns' that formally splits WHAT is protected (assets) from HOW it is attacked (attack paths), which the authors argue existing agentic-AI security frameworks (OWASP, MAESTRO, MITRE ATLAS) fail to do. Three concrete contributions: (1) a human-centric asset taxonomy grounded in 'victim modeling' and inspired by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, defining assets as ultimate human values rather than technical components; (2) a formal graph-based attack-path model that distinguishes 'poison paths' (how a malicious payload reaches the victim) from 'trigger paths' (how the victim is caused to consume the poison), and observes that trigger paths are structurally identical to poison paths, yielding a recursive 'attack within an attack' structure; and (3) an open-source tool, the AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator / Pathfinder, that operationalizes the model to automatically discover and cost-rank multi-stage attacks via a bi-level search.
threat_model: In-vehicle LLM agents (e.g., BMW IPA, VW IDA, Mercedes MBUX) that coordinate with external services through Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. A2A provides transport-layer security (OAuth 2.0, HTTPS) that authenticates the SENDER but not the CONTENT, and makes no protocol-level distinction between human-originated and agent-originated instructions, so both enter the receiving agent's context window with equal privilege. The adversary is an authenticated-but-compromised external agent (or poisoned datasource) that injects arbitrary natural-language payloads via A2A fields (TextPart, FilePart, DataPart, artifact name/description, metadata). Attacks manifest as indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning, confused-deputy tool misuse, and forced-response injection. The automotive context amplifies impact: consequences range from driver distraction to unauthorized vehicle control, and the driver's cognitive load reduces scrutiny. Assets are framed around four victim perspectives (Primary User, Digital/Trust Network, Environmental Spillover, System Owner/Provider), with cascading harms radiating from the primary user as 'epicenter.'
limitations: Stated by the authors: the model assumes a FIXED architecture (no runtime dynamic agent discovery); it is primarily TOPOLOGICAL, reasoning about path existence rather than likelihood; all edges are treated as equally exploitable even though prompt-injection difficulty varies with guardrails; the engine requires explicitly modeled trigger paths, excluding autonomous/ambient consumption (e.g., periodic email polling); and empirical validation against production systems remains future work. Reviewer-noted: the paper reports NO experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative results of its own -- effectiveness of the taxonomy and tool is argued by construction and illustrative scenarios only. The quantitative figures that appear (CAR-bench success rate, 27-second recovery time) are drawn from cited prior work, not measured here.

datasets_used: None. The paper conducts no empirical experiments and uses no datasets of its own. CAR-bench (arXiv:2601.22027) is referenced from cited work [10], not run by the authors.
quantitative_results: The paper reports NO original quantitative results (no experiments, tables of measurements, or benchmarks of its own).
Cited (not the paper's own): frontier reasoning LLMs achieve <50% consistent success on disambiguation tasks in the CAR-bench automotive benchmark [ref 10].
Cited (not the paper's own): cognitive distraction from voice interactions can degrade driving performance for up to 27 seconds after an interaction ends [refs 9,7,22].
Formal construct (not a measurement): trigger actions are unit-cost (cost 1); step cost = Cost(PushPoison) + Cost(ActivationTrigger) + Cost(ConsumptionTrigger); automatic 'watch' consumption costs 1.
Taxonomy sizes: 4 victim perspectives, 7 UDHR-derived asset categories for the primary user, 2 graph node types, 4 edge types.
baselines_compared: No empirical baselines. The paper critiques existing frameworks conceptually rather than benchmarking against them: OWASP Agentic AI Threats [14] (10 categories: 7 attack methods, 2 consequences, 1 conflated), MAESTRO [6] (domain-agnostic, enterprise-oriented), MITRE ATLAS [23] (technique catalogue, non-generative), and ISO/SAE 21434 TARA [1] (deterministic, not adapted to generative AI). Real CVEs CurXecute (CVE-2025-54135) and EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) are used as illustrative case studies of the poison/trigger pattern, not as evaluation baselines.
